title: fix: hide retired models from API key permissions
author: ElliotEtag
pr_body_excerpt:
## Problem

API-key model restrictions are stored as JSON snapshots in D1. Removing a model from the catalog did not update those snapshots, so retired IDs remained visible even though they no longer granted usable access.

## Fix

- Intersects restricted model IDs with the current canonical callable catalog at read time; D1 remains unchanged.
- Applies the projection to dashboard/account lists, current-key status, and update responses.
- Includes built-in models, active public community models, and only the caller's active private community models.
- Preserves empty model allowlists as deny-all across authentication, generation, realtime, and model discovery.
- Skips catalog/D1 enrichment for unrestricted keys.

## Impact

Retired, aliased, and non-canonical IDs disappear from responses while canonical permissions remain visible. A filtered empty list cannot become unrestricted when round-tripped through an update.

## Validation

- Enter and Gen: `npx tsc --noEmit`
- Enter affected files: 55 pass; 4 existing failures use intentionally blocked native Better Auth routes
- Gen permission and realtime files: 22 pass
- Biome and GitHub CI

gist.headline: The retired bees leave quietly
gist.blurb: Old model IDs were lingering in API-key permission lists like labels on empty flowerpots. The dashboard and API now show the callable models that are actually in the garden.
gist.summary: API-key model restrictions are now projected against the canonical callable catalog at read time, hiding retired, aliased, and non-canonical model IDs while preserving empty allowlists as deny-all.
gist.impact: Dashboard lists, current-key status, update responses, generation, realtime, and model discovery now consistently retain only usable canonical permissions, including each caller's active private community models.
gist.visual_concept: Depict an API-key permissions garden where outdated model-name tags gently fade from empty pots while healthy canonical code-plants remain clearly labeled. Polly, acting as a careful garden keeper with a tiny clipboard and pruning shears, checks a glowing key-shaped sign; a CRT monitor robot can display the clean active-model list. The visual metaphor is catalog cleanup without changing the underlying stored soil: only the currently callable flowers are visible.
gist.image_prompt: A cozy pixel art API-key permissions garden where a cute pixel art bee mascot with a yellow body and dark brown horizontal stripes, small black legs, two black antennae, black vertical oval eyes, bright pink rectangular blush marks on cheeks, a small happy smile, and small rounded light blue translucent wings wears a tiny gardener hat and gently prunes faded old model labels from flowerpots, leaving glowing canonical code-flowers beside a lime green (#ecf874) key-shaped sign; a friendly chunky CRT monitor robot shows a tidy active-model list. Cozy 8-bit pixel art aesthetic, soft lime green (#ecf874) and pastel gradient background, warm lighting, retro gaming vibes like Stardew Valley or A Short Hike, with subtle pixel-font text: "ACTIVE MODELS"; nostalgic but beautiful, emotionally warm.
